learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Recursion, Merge Sort, Divide and Conquer, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine students standing according to roll numbers, but some students are out of order. Every pair of students standing in the wrong relative order represents an inversion. Counting inversions tells how far the arrangement is from being sorted.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine students standing according to roll numbers, but some students are out of order. Every pair of students standing in the wrong relative order represents an inversion. Counting inversions tells how far the arrangement is from being sorted.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this Merge Sort counting technique whenever you need to count pair relationships efficiently while sorting.
learning.signals: Whenever a problem asks to count ordered pairs efficiently, especially involving array ordering, think of Merge Sort counting.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: Given an integer array arr, count the number of inversions in the array. An inversion is a pair (i, j) such that i < j and arr[i] > arr[j]. Return the total number of inversions.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Compare every pair of elements and count whenever arr[i] > arr[j].
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Brute force compares every pair, but Merge Sort naturally divides the array. During merging, whenever an element from the right half is placed before an element from the left half, it forms inversions with all remaining elements in the left half.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: When a right element is smaller than a left element during merge, it forms inversions with every remaining element in the left half.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever a problem asks to count ordered pairs efficiently, especially involving array ordering, think of Merge Sort counting.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Merge Sort processes each level in linear time and has log n levels. Counting inversions during merging avoids comparing every pair individually.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever a problem asks to count ordered pairs efficiently, especially involving array ordering, think of Merge Sort counting.
commonWrongPaths: Adding only one inversion instead of all remaining left elements.
Overflowing inversion count by using int.

hints: Count inversions during the merge step.
commonMisconceptions: Adding only one inversion instead of all remaining left elements.
Overflowing inversion count by using int.

pattern.name: Merge Sort
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever a problem asks to count ordered pairs efficiently, especially involving array ordering, think of Merge Sort counting.

approaches.title: Nested Loop Comparison
approaches.idea: Compare every pair of elements and count whenever arr[i] > arr[j].
algorithm: Loop j from i+1 to n-1.

approaches.title: Merge Sort Counting
approaches.idea: Use Merge Sort and count inversions while merging the two sorted halves.
algorithm: Recursively count inversions in both halves.
approaches.dryRun:
Left=[2,4]
Right=[1,3]
1 is smaller than 2 and 4.
Add 2 inversions.

code.java: Uses Merge Sort with inversion counting.

approaches.title: Merge Sort with Inversion Counting
approaches.idea: During Merge Sort, whenever an element from the right subarray is smaller than the current element from the left subarray, all remaining elements in the left subarray form inversions. Add (mid - leftIndex + 1) to the inversion count.
approaches.intuition: Merge Sort processes each level in linear time and has log n levels. Counting inversions during merging avoids comparing every pair individually.
algorithm: Count inversions in the left half.
Count inversions in the right half.
If left element <= right element, copy left.
Otherwise copy right and add remaining left elements to inversion count.
approaches.dryRun:
arr=[2,4,1,3]
Split into [2,4] and [1,3]
During merge:
1 < 2 → Add 2 inversions
3 < 4 → Add 1 inversion
Answer = 3

code.cpp: Uses Merge Sort with inversion counting by adding (mid-i+1) whenever right element is smaller.
code.javascript: Uses Merge Sort recursion and counts remaining left elements during merge.

visualization.description: Count inversions while merging two sorted halves.
steps: Whenever right element is smaller, count remaining left elements.

interviewFollowUps: Can this be solved using a Binary Indexed Tree?
Can Segment Tree solve this problem?
How is this related to Merge Sort?
interviewTips: Mention why Merge Sort is suitable.
Clearly explain why (mid - i + 1) inversions are added.
Use long for large inversion counts.
Discuss alternative BIT/Segment Tree solutions if asked.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: When a right element is smaller than a left element during merge, it forms inversions with every remaining element in the left half.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Merge Sort → Count During Merge → Return Total.

importantPoints: Inversion: i < j and arr[i] > arr[j].
commonMistakes: Adding only one inversion instead of all remaining left elements.
Overflowing inversion count by using int.

relatedProblems: Count of Smaller Numbers After Self
